figures.caption: Table 2. Quantitative segmentation performance (mean ± std) on CHTN, OSU, and Camelyon16 datasets. We compare our proposed method against the classical Nested U-Net (UNet++) baseline [13] and the standard Dino U-Net [4]. Note that "Standard" denotes the original Dino U-Net trained with Dice+CrossEntropy loss, while "Standard + BCE" uses BCE+Dice loss to ensure fair comparison with UNet++ and our method. Bold indicates the best performance, and underline indicates the second-best performance.

Fig. 2. Comparative segmentation results on CHTN (rows 1–2), OSU (rows 3–4), and CAMELYON16 (rows 5–6), highlighting infiltrative tumor boundaries. Columns: (a) Input patch, (b) Ground truth, (c) UNet++ [13], (d) Dino-UNet (Standard) [4], (e) Dino-UNet (BCE), and (f) Ours (Dino-NestedUNet). Red and Yellow dashed boxes indicate false positives and false negatives, respectively. Compared with prior methods, our model suppresses artifacts and more accurately follows complex tumor boundaries.

Fig. 4. Distribution of Dice Similarity Coefficients. Boxplots illustrating the performance spread of (a) UNet++, (b) Standard Dino-UNet, (c) Dino-UNet (BCE), and (d) Dino-NestedUNet across the three testing cohorts.

Fig. 3. Representative whole-slide segmentation results on the CHTN cohort. Representative CHTN whole-slide images showing (a) the original WSI (Hematoxylin & Eosin stain); (b–c) segmentation masks (white = tumor, black = non-tumor background): (b) ground truth and (c) ours (Dino-NestedUNet).
